title: How I stopped 100 goroutines from hammering my gRPC server — Loom Part 2
description:
This is Part 2 of my series building Loom.

👉 Missed Part 1? Read it here

Today: Reflection cache, stampede protection, and the deadlock that kept me up until 11 PM.

The problem

When 50 goroutines all need the same method descriptor at the same time, my naive code made ALL 50 hit the backend:

func (c *ReflectionCache) GetMethod(method string) (*MethodDescriptor, error) {
    return c.fetchFromBackend(method)  // 🔥 50x RPC calls
}

Result: 50 identical calls. 50x load. 50x latency. Not good.

The fix: singleflight

Go has singleflight in golang.org/x/sync — it ensures only one goroutine fetches, the rest wait for that result.

Final code: 

import "golang.org/x/sync/singleflight"

type ReflectionCache struct {
    cache map[string]*MethodDescriptor
    mu    sync.RWMutex
    group singleflight.Group
}

func (c *ReflectionCache) GetMethod(method string) (*MethodDescriptor, error) {
    // Fast path: already cached?
    c.mu.RLock()
    if desc, ok := c.cache[method]; ok {
        c.mu.RUnlock()
        return desc, nil
    }
    c.mu.RUnlock()

    // Slow path: single fetch, everyone waits
    result, err, _ := c.group.Do(method, func() (interface{}, error) {
        desc, err := c.fetchFromBackend(method)
        if err != nil {
            return nil, err
        }
        c.mu.Lock()
        c.cache[method] = desc
        c.mu.Unlock()
        return desc, nil
    })

    return result.(*MethodDescriptor), err
}

What changed: 1 backend call instead of 50. All 50 goroutines get the result in ~50ms instead of 2500ms.

The embarrassing deadlock

I tried building this myself first. Here's the bug that took 3 hours:

// ⚠️ DEADLOCK — Don't do this
func (c *ReflectionCache) GetMethod(method string) (*MethodDescriptor, error) {
    c.mu.Lock()
    defer c.mu.Unlock()  // ❌ This will run later

    // ... check cache ...

    c.mu.Unlock()  // Manual unlock
    desc, _ := c.fetchFromBackend(method)
    c.mu.Lock()    // Re-lock

    return desc, nil  // defer still tries to unlock → panic
}

Lesson: Don't mix defer and manual lock/unlock. And just use singleflight.

Performance

Approach Backend calls (100 reqs) Total time
No cache 100 5000ms
Mutex only 1 5000ms
Singleflight 1 ~52ms
96% faster.

Key takeaways

Cache stampedes are real — they'll crush your backend
singleflight is your friend — don't roll your own
Test with -race — it catches deadlocks
Read locks (RLock) for cache hits — saves contention

Try Loom yourself

joshuabvarghese / Loom

gRPC L7 Debugging Proxy

Loom

A gRPC debugging proxy. Point it at your backend, point your client at Loom, and watch every call decoded in a browser tab.

Your gRPC Client  →  Loom (:9999)  →  Your Backend (:50051)
                          ↓
                    Web Inspector
                  http://localhost:9998

Why

gRPC traffic is binary. Wireshark can't read it. grpcurl is great for one-off calls but you can't watch a flow. I kept running it over and over trying to understand what was happening between services.

Loom sits transparently between your client and backend. It uses Server Reflection to decode every frame on the fly — no .proto files required — and streams the results into a browser UI. You see the JSON payloads, the status codes, how long each call took, and a ready-to-copy grpcurl command to replay any of them.

What it does

  • Intercepts all four gRPC stream types — unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, bidi
  • Auto-decodes using Server Reflection (no proto…


View on GitHub

title: Building a Financial Risk Intelligence Agent That Learns from Every Investigation
description:
Enhancing Fraud Investigations Through Memory-Powered AI Agents

Traditional fraud detection systems are excellent at identifying suspicious transactions, but they have one major limitation:

They don't remember.

Every transaction is treated as a brand-new event. The model generates a score, the analyst reviews the case, and once the investigation is complete, all the valuable knowledge gained during that process disappears.

After building several fraud detection systems, I realized the biggest problem wasn't model accuracy—it was the lack of memory.

So I built a Financial Risk Intelligence Agent that learns from every investigation.

Instead of relying only on risk scores, the system retrieves similar historical investigations before making recommendations, allowing the agent to reason using past experience.

The Problem with Traditional Fraud Detection

A typical fraud detection workflow looks like this:

Transaction → ML Model → Risk Score → Alert → Analyst Review → Case Closed

This approach works well for detecting known patterns, but it ignores something critical:

Analyst expertise.

Experienced fraud investigators don't make decisions based solely on scores.

They ask:

  • Have we seen this pattern before?

  • Was it confirmed fraud?

  • Was it a false positive?

  • What actions resolved the case?

  • What indicators mattered most?

Traditional systems cannot answer these questions because they have no memory.

The Core Idea

Instead of asking:

"How risky is this transaction?"

The system asks:

"Have we seen something similar before, and what did we learn from it?"

That small shift transforms a fraud detector into an intelligence system.

System Architecture

The solution consists of four layers.

1. Transaction Analysis Layer

This layer extracts transaction features such as:

  • Transaction amount

  • Geography

  • Device fingerprint

  • Merchant category

  • Transaction timing

These features provide the context needed for investigation.

2. Fraud Detection Engine

The extracted features are passed to a machine learning model.

The model generates:

  • Risk score

  • Risk category

  • Confidence level

Example:

  • Risk Score: 77%

  • Category: High Risk

  • Confidence: 91%

This is the standard component found in most fraud detection systems.

3. Memory Layer (The Key Innovation)

This is where the system becomes different.

Instead of storing raw transactions, it stores investigation outcomes and lessons learned.

Each memory contains:

  • Fraud type

  • Transaction characteristics

  • Risk indicators

  • Analyst decision

  • Investigation summary

  • Resolution steps

  • Final outcome

When a new transaction arrives, the system performs semantic similarity search and retrieves the most relevant historical investigations.

The agent receives context before making a recommendation.

4. AI Investigation Agent

The agent combines:

  • Current transaction data

  • Risk score

  • Historical memories

It then generates a complete investigation report with reasoning and recommendations.

Instead of producing a number, it produces actionable intelligence.

Real-World Example

Incoming Transaction

  • Amount: ₹475,000

  • Location: Dubai

  • Type: Wire Transfer

  • Time: 01:45 AM

Model Output

  • Risk Score: 72%

  • Confidence: 91%

Without memory, the investigation ends here.

The analyst simply sees:

High Risk Transaction

What Happens with Memory?

The system retrieves:

  • 3 previously confirmed fraud cases

  • 1 similar false-positive case

The false-positive case involved a customer who had an active travel notice on file.

The agent now generates:

Risk Score: 72%. Three previously confirmed fraud cases match this transaction profile. One similar case was a false positive due to an active travel notice. Recommendation: Freeze transaction pending verification and check travel records before contacting the customer.

Same model.

Completely different investigation quality.

The Learning Loop

The most important part of the architecture is the feedback loop.

Step 1

Transaction arrives.

Step 2

ML model generates a risk score.

Step 3

Memory layer retrieves similar historical investigations.

Step 4

AI agent creates a contextual investigation report.

Step 5

Analyst confirms the outcome.

Step 6

The outcome is written back into memory.

Every completed investigation becomes training data for future investigations.

The system continuously improves through experience.

What Changed After Adding Memory?

The improvement wasn't just accuracy.

The behavior of the entire system changed.

Before Memory

  • Relied almost entirely on risk scores

  • Generic recommendations

  • Limited explainability

  • Low analyst trust

After Memory

  • Referenced historical cases

  • Provided evidence-backed recommendations

  • Better handling of false positives

  • More contextual reasoning

  • Higher analyst confidence

The biggest difference was trust.

Analysts were far more willing to follow recommendations when those recommendations were supported by previous cases rather than a single percentage score.

Key Lessons

1. Memory Can Be More Valuable Than Model Improvements

Small gains in model accuracy often produce less impact than adding historical context.

Experience matters.

2. Analyst Knowledge Should Not Be Lost

Every investigation contains valuable information.

A memory layer turns analyst decisions into reusable organizational intelligence.

3. Explainability Drives Adoption

People trust systems that can explain their reasoning.

Evidence-backed recommendations outperform black-box predictions.

4. Feedback Loops Create Compounding Value

Every completed investigation improves future investigations.

The system becomes more useful over time.

5. Fraud Evolves Constantly

Static models struggle with new attack patterns.

Memory allows the system to adapt much faster by learning from newly confirmed cases.

Future Improvements

Some enhancements I plan to explore include:

  • Time-weighted memory decay

  • Specialized memory stores for different fraud categories

  • Multi-agent investigation workflows

  • Confidence-based memory ranking

  • Graph-based relationship analysis

Final Thoughts

Machine learning models are excellent at detecting anomalies.

But anomalies alone are not intelligence.

What transforms detection into investigation is memory.

By combining machine learning, retrieval systems, and analyst feedback loops, we can build AI systems that learn the way experienced investigators do—through accumulated experience.

The future of financial intelligence isn't just better models.

It's systems that remember.

Building AI systems that learn from experience, not just data.

title: GA Power Pages AI Skills Under Test: Better Scaffolding, Same Local Auth Wall
description:
TL;DR: building a Power Pages SPA with the GitHub Copilot AI skills for Power Platform, the portal handles authentication in a completely different way from the previous article — custom React forms, server-side form scraping, CSRF token fetching, session resolution via /_services/auth/user. And yet, when running locally, it hits exactly the same wall: the portal set to "Private" redirects every request through Entra, with a redirect_uri that points to the production URL. The fix is the same: set the portal to "Public".

📖 Background: experimenting with the AI Skills for Power Pages SPA

Microsoft recently released the AI skills for building Power Pages portals to GA, and that made them worth revisiting seriously. Compared to the earlier preview, the GA wave brings more commands, broader end-to-end operations, and much better support for authentication models beyond Entra.

So I did what I usually do when I want to understand a tool properly: I tried to build a portal from scratch using only the Microsoft-provided skills.

The test case was a ticket management portal: external users register, open tickets, track progress, and exchange comments with a support team. The data model I built the usual way, with PACX, because I still find it significantly more effective for that part of the job. The rest — portal, authentication, and Web API integration — was generated through the skills, with me acting mainly as the person defining requirements, reviewing the plan, and approving each step.

This article is not the promised deep dive into the skills themselves. It focuses on a very specific issue that showed up during local development, and that turned out to have exactly the same root cause as the one I described last week, despite a very different authentication implementation.

🧱 How this portal handles authentication

The AI skills generated a local authentication setup that is meaningfully different from the handwritten code I described in the previous article. Worth walking through it, because the implementation choices are non-obvious.

All the code below was scaffolded by asking Copilot:

/setup-auth with local authentication and a basic registration form

🔐 The login form

The portal renders its own custom React login form at /login.

There is no redirect to a Power Pages-hosted login page, and no embedded iframe. The form collects email and password client-side, then executes a multi-step sequence:

Step 1 — Fetch a CSRF token.

export async function fetchAntiForgeryToken(): Promise<string> {
    const response = await fetch('/_layout/tokenhtml');
    // ...parses the HTML response and extracts the value="..." from the hidden input
    return match[1];
}

Power Pages exposes /_layout/tokenhtml, a small HTML fragment containing a hidden __RequestVerificationToken field. The token must be included in every mutating request or the server rejects it with 400.

Step 2 — POST to /SignIn.

const body = new URLSearchParams();
body.set('__RequestVerificationToken', token);
body.set('Email', credential);
body.set('PasswordValue', password);
body.set('ReturnUrl', returnUrl || '/');

const response = await fetch('/SignIn', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' },
    body: body.toString(),
    credentials: 'same-origin',
    redirect: 'follow',
});

/SignIn is the Power Pages native local authentication endpoint. On success it sets a session cookie and redirects to ReturnUrl. The React handler detects the redirect and navigates the SPA accordingly.

Step 3 — Resolve the user identity.

On the deployed portal, window.Microsoft.Dynamic365.Portal.User is injected by Power Pages' server-rendered HTML and is immediately available. When running locally via Vite, however, that object is never present — the Vite dev server serves the HTML, not Power Pages.

To bridge the gap, useAuth falls back to an async fetch. The AI skills initially scaffolded this as a clean JSON call:

// What the AI skills generated — does NOT actually work
const resp = await fetch('/_services/auth/user', { credentials: 'same-origin' });
const data = await resp.json(); // ❌ throws — the response is not JSON

But /_services/auth/user does not return JSON. It returns a full HTML page — the Power Pages portal shell — with the user data embedded as a JavaScript object inside a <script> block:

<script>
  window.Microsoft = window.Microsoft || {};
  window.Microsoft.Dynamic365 = window.Microsoft.Dynamic365 || {};
  window.Microsoft.Dynamic365.Portal = window.Microsoft.Dynamic365.Portal || {};
  window.Microsoft.Dynamic365.Portal.User = {
    contactId: 'xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx',
    userName: 'riccardo.gregori@outlook.com',
    email: 'riccardo.gregori@outlook.com',
    firstName: 'Riccardo',
    lastName: 'Gregori',
    userRoles: ["Authenticated Users"],
  };
</script>

Even when the route nominally returns a 404 (the page title says "Page not found"), the shell still injects the user object if the session is authenticated. The presence of contactId is the authenticated signal.

The fix was to rewrite fetchCurrentUser to parse the HTML response with regex:

export async function fetchCurrentUser(): Promise<PowerPagesUser | undefined> {
    const platformUser = window.Microsoft?.Dynamic365?.Portal?.User;
    if (platformUser?.userName) return platformUser;

    const resp = await fetch('/_services/auth/user', { credentials: 'same-origin' });
    if (!resp.ok) return undefined;
    const html = await resp.text();

    // Extract the embedded JS object from the page shell
    const contactIdMatch = html.match(/contactId:\s*['"]([^'"]+)['"]/);
    if (!contactIdMatch) return undefined; // not authenticated

    const extract = (key: string) =>
        html.match(new RegExp(`${key}:\\s*['"]([^'"]*)['"']`))?.[1] ?? '';
    const rolesMatch = html.match(/userRoles:\s*\[([^\]]*)\]/);
    const userRoles = rolesMatch
        ? rolesMatch[1].replace(/['"]/g, '').split(',').map(r => r.trim()).filter(Boolean)
        : ['Authenticated Users'];

    return {
        contactId: contactIdMatch[1],
        userName: extract('userName'),
        email:     extract('email'),
        firstName: extract('firstName'),
        lastName:  extract('lastName'),
        userRoles,
    };
}

I couldn't find /_services/auth/user documented on Microsoft Learn. In practice it requires no table permissions and is reachable via the Vite proxy. It cannot be called unsupported — it's the AI skills from Microsoft themselves that scaffolded the call.

📝 Registration

Registration is handled entirely via server-side form scraping — a technique that reflects the reality of Power Pages local auth:

  1. Fetch the registration page HTML from /Account/Login/Register.
  2. Parse it as a DOM document and extract the form, the hidden ASP.NET fields (__VIEWSTATE, __EVENTVALIDATION, etc.), and the anti-forgery token.
  3. POST the form to its action URL with the user's details.
  4. Follow the redirect on success, or parse server-side validation errors from the response HTML.

There is no JSON API for registration. The portal's native registration form is ASP.NET WebForms under the hood, and the only way to drive it programmatically from a React SPA is to scrape and replay its state. The skills did this correctly — they introspect the live form before submitting, which means the implementation handles variations in field names and form actions without hardcoding them.

The Vite proxy

To make all of this work locally, the vite.config.ts defines proxies for the relevant paths and strips the Secure and Domain attributes from cookies (plus rewrites SameSite=None to SameSite=Lax and absolute Location headers to relative paths):

function rewriteProxyResponse(proxyRes) {
    const setCookie = proxyRes.headers['set-cookie'];
    if (setCookie) {
        proxyRes.headers['set-cookie'] = setCookie.map((cookie) =>
            cookie
                .replace(/;\s*Secure/gi, '')
                .replace(/;\s*Domain=[^;]*/gi, '')
                .replace(/SameSite=None/gi, 'SameSite=Lax'),
        );
    }
    const location = proxyRes.headers['location'];
    if (typeof location === 'string' && location.startsWith(PORTAL_TARGET)) {
        proxyRes.headers['location'] = location.slice(PORTAL_TARGET.length) || '/';
    }
}

This is necessary because session cookies issued by nn-ticketing.powerappsportals.com would otherwise be rejected by the browser on http://localhost.

Note: I had to ask GitHub Copilot to properly configure proxies. By default, ootb AI skills set up your portal with the following basic vite.config.ts:

import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react';

export default defineConfig({
    plugins: [react()],
    build: {
        outDir: 'dist',
    },
});

That suffers from all the issues I've already described in my previous posts. After asking GH Copilot to add proper proxy management, the vite.config.ts changed like this:

import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react';
import type { IncomingMessage } from 'http';

// Strip Secure flag and Domain from Set-Cookie headers so that session cookies
// issued by the proxied Power Pages site are accepted by the browser on localhost.
function stripSecureCookies(proxyRes: IncomingMessage) {
    const setCookie = proxyRes.headers['set-cookie'];
    if (setCookie) {
        proxyRes.headers['set-cookie'] = setCookie.map((cookie) =>
            cookie
                .replace(/;\s*Secure/gi, '')
                .replace(/;\s*Domain=[^;]*/gi, '')
                .replace(/SameSite=None/gi, 'SameSite=Lax'),
        );
    }
}

const PORTAL_TARGET = 'https://REDACTED.powerappsportals.com';

const proxyBase = {
    target: PORTAL_TARGET,
    changeOrigin: true,
    secure: true,
};

const proxyWithCookies = {
    ...proxyBase,
    configure: (proxy: import('http-proxy').Server) => {
        proxy.on('proxyRes', (proxyRes) => stripSecureCookies(proxyRes));
    },
};

export default defineConfig({
    plugins: [react()],
    build: {
        outDir: 'dist',
        rollupOptions: {
            output: {
                entryFileNames: `assets/[name].js`,
                chunkFileNames: `assets/[name].js`,
                assetFileNames: `assets/[name].[ext]`,
                manualChunks: {
                    router: ['react-router-dom'],
                },
            },
        },
    },
    server: {
        proxy: {
            '/_api': proxyWithCookies,
            '/_layout': proxyWithCookies,
            '/_services': proxyWithCookies,
            '/Account': proxyWithCookies,
            '/SignIn': proxyWithCookies,
        },
    },
});

🐛 The problem

With all of this in place, running npm run dev and navigating to http://localhost:5173/login showed the React login form. Filling in valid credentials and submitting returned:

Failed to fetch

Playwright confirmed the network behaviour:

HTTP 302  http://localhost:5173/SignIn
HTTP 302  http://localhost:5173/_api/contacts?...
CONSOLE ERROR: Access to fetch at 'https://login.microsoftonline.com/...'
  (redirected from 'http://localhost:5173/SignIn') from origin 'http://localhost:5173'
  has been blocked by CORS policy: No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present.

Every proxied request — POST /SignIn, GET /_layout/tokenhtml, GET /_api/contacts — was returning a 302 to https://login.microsoftonline.com. None of the application code was executing at all.

The redirect URL contained:

redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Fnn-ticketing.powerappsportals.com%2F

The portal was configured as "Private" in the Power Pages admin center.

🔁 Same root cause, different authentication stack

In the previous article, the portal used a minimal auth stack: a fetchUser() function that read from window.Microsoft.Dynamic365.Portal.User or fell back to /_services/auth/user, and login redirected to /Account/SignIn via a full page navigation. The implementation was handwritten and relatively simple.

This portal's auth stack is substantially more involved: custom React forms, CSRF token fetching, server-side form scraping, SPA-native POST to /SignIn, cookie rewriting in the Vite proxy, and an async fetchCurrentUser fallback. The AI skills generated all of it correctly.

None of it mattered. The "Private" flag sits upstream of every endpoint — /SignIn, /_layout/tokenhtml, /_services/auth/user, /_api/* — and intercepts all of them with the same Entra gate before any application-level logic can run. The sophistication of the authentication implementation is irrelevant: if the portal is Private, the Entra redirect blocks the flow at the network level, and the redirect_uri points to the production URL, not localhost.

The only variable that matters for local development is:

Is the portal set to "Public" or "Private" in the Power Pages admin center?

If "Private": every local request gets a 302 to login.microsoftonline.com with a redirect_uri you cannot override.

If "Public": all proxied requests reach the application layer, cookies are set correctly, and the local auth flow works.

✅ The solution (part 1)

Power Pages admin center → Site Details → switch from "Private" to "Public". Then https://make.powerpages.microsoft.com/ → your site → Site visibility → Public - when you're ready to go live.

That's it for the network layer. Wait half an hour for the portal to wake up after changing that config, and the proxied requests start reaching the application.

🐛 The second problem: isAuthenticated always false

After setting the portal to Public, login no longer failed. Playwright confirmed the URL became /tickets after submitting valid credentials. But the navbar still showed "Sign in" — as if the user was not authenticated.

The root cause was a design mismatch in useAuth:

// What the hook returned on every render:
return {
    isAuthenticated: checkAuth(),       // ← synchronous platform check
    displayName:     getUserDisplayName(), // ← also synchronous
    initials:        getUserInitials(),    // ← also synchronous
    // ...
}

checkAuth() (and the two display helpers) all call getCurrentUser(), which reads window.Microsoft.Dynamic365.Portal.User synchronously. On the deployed portal, as previously mentioned, this object is injected by Power Pages' server-rendered HTML and is available immediately. On Vite, you must fill in "manually".

Even though fetchCurrentUser() was correctly resolving the user from /_services/auth/user HTML parsing and calling setUser(resolved), the hook recomputed isAuthenticated from the synchronous platform check on every render — always false.

✅ The solution (part 2)

The fix was to stop calling the synchronous helpers in the return statement and derive everything from the React user state:

return {
    isAuthenticated: !!user?.userName,   // derived from state
    displayName: user
        ? [user.firstName, user.lastName].filter(Boolean).join(' ') || user.email || 'User'
        : '',
    initials: user
        ? ((user.firstName?.[0] ?? '') + (user.lastName?.[0] ?? '')).toUpperCase() || user.userName?.[0]?.toUpperCase() ?? ''
        : '',
    // ...
}

Once fetchCurrentUser() resolves and setUser() is called, React re-renders with the correct values.

To recap, the useAuth hook must derive all user-dependent values from the React user state, never from synchronous platform object reads. The async fetchCurrentUser() fallback is meaningless if the return values bypass it on every render.

This is a subtlety that is easy to miss: the code looks correct (there is an async fallback, it runs, it calls setUser()), but the derived values are computed from a different, synchronous path that ignores the state update.

🧐 Conclusions

Building a Power Pages SPA with AI skills changes the implementation experience considerably — the auth stack, the Web API integration, the table permissions, and the deployment pipeline were all set up correctly without manual implementation work.

Using the GA Power Pages AI skills on a real implementation left me frankly astonished by how much manual work was removed from the critical path. A large amount of repetitive, error-prone setup that normally burns days was generated in minutes, and that materially changed the pace of delivery.

At the same time, this experiment reinforced the most important truth in our job: no matter how good the tooling gets, the person between the chair and the keyboard is still the real differentiator. AI is fast, but it is sometimes wrong, sometimes incomplete, and sometimes confidently wrong in exactly the places where production experience matters most.

So my takeaway is simple: keep studying, keep experimenting, and keep failing on purpose in controlled contexts. Field knowledge is still what lets you recognize bad assumptions quickly, validate what the tool produced, and turn speed into actual effectiveness instead of accidental complexity.

title: R2D2 Creation with Codey 🤖 Ep.8
description:
Episode 8: The Complete Droid

"The Force Will Be With You. Always." 🌟

The entire crew stands in the workshop. Han Solo, arms folded. Luke, grinning. C-3PO, hands clasped with barely-contained anxiety. Princess Leia's message loops on the OLED — a recreation, text only, but the words are right: "Help me, Obi-Wan Kenobi."

And in the centre, dome slowly rotating, blue-white LEDs breathing, speaker playing a quiet ambient whistle — R2-D2.

OBI-WAN: quietly "Seven episodes. Seven systems. And now, the droid is nearly complete. There is one thing left — the final integration. The moment when all systems stop running in parallel and begin running as one coherent whole. When R2-D2 stops being a collection of components and becomes the galaxy's greatest droid."

R2-D2 beeps once. Confident. Ready.

OBI-WAN: "And then — the Force. The Wi-Fi."

Han Solo snorts.

HAN: "The Wi-Fi. Very mystical."

🗂️ SIPOC — The Complete Droid

Suppliers Inputs Process Outputs Customers
You (the maker) "Integrate all systems + add Wi-Fi remote command interface" Codey Deep Think mode plans the full architecture before writing A unified state machine with all systems communicating, Wi-Fi endpoint for remote commands The complete R2-D2 — one firmware, all systems orchestrated
ESP32-S3 N16R8 16MB Flash, 8MB PSRAM, dual-core, Wi-Fi Dual-core operation: Core 0 for Wi-Fi/networking, Core 1 for real-time sensor/motor/audio loop Smooth operation without Wi-Fi blocking the real-time control loop All R2-D2 systems — none starved of processor time
Codey Deep Think mode Complex multi-file integration prompt Takes longer to respond — reasons about the whole architecture first The cleanest, most architecturally coherent code in the series Your long-term maintainability
Final Wiring Diagram All 7 systems' component lists Codey draws the complete master diagram with all connections, protection circuits, and power rails A single PDF showing the complete R2-D2 build Your workshop wall — the crown jewel of your build log

The Final Component Inventory — Every Piece of R2-D2 🔧

C-3PO produces a datapad with the air of someone who has been waiting seven episodes to read this list.

C-3PO: "The complete inventory, as accumulated across eight glorious — and at times terrifying — episodes:"

Component Episode Added Purpose
ESP32-S3 N16R8 5 The brain — all systems connect here
SSD1306 OLED 128×64 5 Holographic display — I2C via GPIO8/9
NeoPixel ring 12 LED 3 Dome lights — GPIO6 via level shifter
HC-SR04 ultrasonic 4 Distance sensing eye — GPIO9/10
PIR HC-SR501 4 Motion sensing — GPIO2 (interrupt)
SG90 servo 6 Dome rotation — GPIO14 via level shifter
DC gear motors × 2 6 Wheel drive — via L298N
L298N motor driver 6 Motor control, also provides 5V rail
DFPlayer Mini 7 Audio playback — UART2 GPIO16/17
Speaker 4Ω/8Ω 7 Voice output
microSD card (FAT32) 7 Audio files 0001–0007.mp3
74AHCT125 level shifter 3/6 NeoPixel + servo: 3.3V → 5V
470Ω resistor 3 NeoPixel data protection
1kΩ resistor 7 DFPlayer RX protection
100Ω resistor — (buzzer, now removed)
100µF capacitor 3 NeoPixel power decoupling
10µF capacitor 7 DFPlayer power filtering
Voltage dividers (10k+20k) 5 HC-SR04 ECHO + PIR output: 5V → 3.3V
9V battery or 2S LiPo 6 Motor power rail
USB cable All Programming + logic power

C-3PO: "R2-D2. In components. On a breadboard. I find this both impressive and somewhat unsettling."

R2-D2 beeps with what sounds like pride.

Deep Think Mode: The Wisest Path 🧠

Yoda settles into his chair.

YODA: "Complex this prompt is. Many files to touch. Many systems to unify. For this — Deep Think mode you shall use. Longer it takes. Wiser the result."

LUKE: "What's Deep Think mode?"

YODA: "Plan mode's elder sibling. Not just a plan — a deep analysis of the architecture first. Consider edge cases it will. Identify conflicts between systems it shall. Only then write, it does."

In Codey, click the mode dropdown and select Deep Think (Pro feature). Then type the final integration prompt:

FINAL R2-D2 INTEGRATION — Deep Think mode

We have these existing header files for the ESP32-S3 N16R8:
  - animations.h  (NeoPixel dome: GPIO6 via 74AHCT125)
  - sensors.h     (HC-SR04: GPIO9 TRIG / GPIO10 ECHO; PIR: GPIO2)
  - display.h     (SSD1306 OLED: I2C SDA=GPIO8 / SCL=GPIO9 — NOTE: conflict!)
  - motion.h      (Servo: GPIO14; L298N: GPIO25-27, GPIO32-34)
  - voice.h       (DFPlayer Mini: UART2 TX=GPIO17 / RX=GPIO16)
  - sounds.h      (LEDC buzzer: now removed, GPIO freed)

CONFLICT TO RESOLVE:
  HC-SR04 TRIG uses GPIO9. OLED I2C SCL also uses GPIO9.
  These cannot share! Remap one of them.
  Suggestion: Move HC-SR04 TRIG to GPIO11, ECHO to GPIO12.
  Update sensors.h and the wiring diagram accordingly.

NEW FEATURE — Wi-Fi remote control:
  Use ESP32-S3 Wi-Fi to create a simple HTTP server.
  Endpoints:
    GET /status    → JSON with distance, motion, dome angle, current sound
    GET /command/forward   → drive forward for 2 seconds
    GET /command/backward  → drive backward for 2 seconds
    GET /command/left      → turn left for 1 second
    GET /command/right     → turn right for 1 second
    GET /command/stop      → stop motors immediately
    GET /command/sound/N   → play sound N (1-7)
    GET /command/dome/N    → move dome to angle N degrees

  Use WiFiAP mode (R2-D2 creates its own access point):
    SSID: "R2-D2"
    Password: "beepboop"

  Run HTTP server on Core 0. Real-time systems on Core 1.

FINAL r2d2-main.ino:
  - Resolve the GPIO conflict
  - Add wifi.h header for Wi-Fi + HTTP server
  - Clean unified loop with clear comments
  - All systems operational
  - Serial output at 115200 showing all state transitions

Please think through the architecture fully before writing.

Deep Think Mode Analysis (Codey Responds) 

[Deep Think analysis in progress...]

Architecture review:

1. GPIO CONFLICT IDENTIFIED:
   GPIO9 cannot serve both HC-SR04 TRIG and I2C SCL.
   Resolution: Remap I2C to SDA=GPIO4, SCL=GPIO5 (available on ESP32-S3).
   This avoids touching sensors.h; only display.h changes:
     Wire.begin(4, 5) instead of Wire.begin(8, 9).

2. DUAL-CORE STRATEGY:
   Core 0 (Arduino default): Wi-Fi, HTTP server, WebServer library
   Core 1 (pinned task): sensor reading, NeoPixel, servo, display, audio
   Separation prevents Wi-Fi TCP/IP stack from blocking 50Hz servo updates.
   Implementation: xTaskCreatePinnedToCore() for real-time loop.

3. STATE MACHINE:
   Unified DomeState enum: IDLE, SCAN, ALERT, DANGER, REMOTE_CONTROL
   REMOTE_CONTROL state: Wi-Fi command overrides sensor-driven behavior.
   Auto-return to IDLE after 5 seconds of no remote command.

4. MEMORY:
   16MB Flash — no concern.
   8MB PSRAM — HTTP response buffers can use ps_malloc() if needed.
   RAM: estimated ~68KB used (20.8% of 327KB) — safe.

5. POWER:
   Wi-Fi radio: ~240mA peak. USB 500mA should be sufficient for ESP32-S3.
   Motors: separate 9V rail — no impact on ESP32 power.

Generating files now...

Generated wifi.h 

// wifi.h — R2-D2 Wi-Fi Remote Control
// ESP32-S3 Access Point: SSID "R2-D2", password "beepboop"
// HTTP server on port 80 — runs on Core 0

#pragma once
#include <WiFi.h>
#include <WebServer.h>
#include <ArduinoJson.h>

const char* AP_SSID     = "R2-D2";
const char* AP_PASSWORD = "beepboop";
const int   HTTP_PORT   = 80;

WebServer server(HTTP_PORT);

// Shared state (volatile — accessed from both cores)
volatile float  sharedDistance    = 999.0f;
volatile bool   sharedMotion      = false;
volatile int    sharedDomeAngle   = 90;
volatile bool   remoteCommandPending = false;
volatile String remoteCommand     = "";

// ── Status endpoint ──────────────────────────────────────────────
void handleStatus() {
  StaticJsonDocument<256> doc;
  doc["distance_cm"] = sharedDistance;
  doc["motion"]      = sharedMotion;
  doc["dome_angle"]  = sharedDomeAngle;
  doc["state"]       = "online";
  doc["uptime_s"]    = millis() / 1000;

  String response;
  serializeJson(doc, response);
  server.send(200, "application/json", response);
  Serial.println("[WiFi] GET /status");
}

// ── Command endpoints ────────────────────────────────────────────
void handleCommand() {
  String path    = server.uri();      // e.g. "/command/forward"
  String command = path.substring(9); // strip "/command/"
  remoteCommand        = command;
  remoteCommandPending = true;
  server.send(200, "text/plain", "ACK: " + command);
  Serial.print("[WiFi] Command: ");
  Serial.println(command);
}

// ── Root page (simple UI) ─────────────────────────────────────────
void handleRoot() {
  String html = R"rawhtml(
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head><title>R2-D2 Control</title>
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1">
<style>
  body { font-family:sans-serif; background:#111; color:#4af;
         display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; }
  h1 { color:#4af; }
  button { background:#1a3a5a; color:#4af; border:2px solid #4af;
           padding:14px 28px; font-size:1.1em; margin:6px;
           border-radius:6px; cursor:pointer; }
  button:active { background:#4af; color:#111; }
  #status { margin-top:16px; font-size:0.9em; color:#aaa; }
</style></head>
<body>
<h1>R2-D2</h1>
<div>
  <button onclick="cmd('sound/5')">🔊 Startup</button>
  <button onclick="cmd('sound/1')">😄 Happy</button>
  <button onclick="cmd('sound/3')">😢 Sad</button>
</div>
<div>
  <button onclick="cmd('forward')">▲ Forward</button>
</div>
<div>
  <button onclick="cmd('left')">◄ Left</button>
  <button onclick="cmd('stop')">■ Stop</button>
  <button onclick="cmd('right')">► Right</button>
</div>
<div>
  <button onclick="cmd('backward')">▼ Backward</button>
</div>
<div>
  <button onclick="cmd('dome/0')">Dome ◄</button>
  <button onclick="cmd('dome/90')">Dome ●</button>
  <button onclick="cmd('dome/180')">Dome ►</button>
</div>
<div id="status">Connecting...</div>
<script>
function cmd(c) {
  fetch('/command/'+c).then(r=>r.text()).then(t=>{
    document.getElementById('status').textContent = 'Last: '+c;
  });
}
setInterval(()=>{
  fetch('/status').then(r=>r.json()).then(d=>{
    document.getElementById('status').textContent =
      'Dist: '+d.distance_cm.toFixed(0)+'cm | Motion: '+d.motion+
      ' | Uptime: '+d.uptime_s+'s';
  });
}, 2000);
</script>
</body></html>
)rawhtml";
  server.send(200, "text/html", html);
}

// ── Init Wi-Fi + HTTP server (call from Core 0 task) ─────────────
void initWifi() {
  WiFi.softAP(AP_SSID, AP_PASSWORD);
  Serial.print("[WiFi] AP started: ");
  Serial.println(AP_SSID);
  Serial.print("[WiFi] IP: ");
  Serial.println(WiFi.softAPIP());

  server.on("/",          handleRoot);
  server.on("/status",    handleStatus);
  server.onNotFound([]() {
    // Any /command/* path
    if (server.uri().startsWith("/command/")) handleCommand();
    else server.send(404, "text/plain", "Not found");
  });

  server.begin();
  Serial.println("[WiFi] HTTP server started — connect to R2-D2 network");
}

// ── Core 0 task: runs Wi-Fi server continuously ──────────────────
void wifiTask(void* param) {
  initWifi();
  for (;;) {
    server.handleClient();
    vTaskDelay(1); // yield to RTOS — must not block Core 0
  }
}

Final r2d2-main.ino 

// r2d2-main.ino — R2-D2 COMPLETE
// ESP32-S3 N16R8 | All systems operational
// Core 0: Wi-Fi + HTTP server
// Core 1: Sensors, display, NeoPixel, servo, motors, audio

#include <Adafruit_NeoPixel.h>
#include "animations.h"   // NeoPixel dome: GPIO6
#include "sensors.h"      // HC-SR04: GPIO9(TRIG) GPIO10(ECHO); PIR: GPIO2
#include "display.h"      // OLED I2C: SDA=GPIO4 SCL=GPIO5 (CONFLICT RESOLVED)
#include "motion.h"       // Servo: GPIO14; L298N: GPIO25-27, GPIO32-34
#include "voice.h"        // DFPlayer: UART2 GPIO16/17
#include "wifi.h"         // Wi-Fi AP + HTTP server

TaskHandle_t realtimeTask;   // Core 1 handle

// ── Remote command processor ─────────────────────────────────────
void processRemoteCommand(const String& cmd) {
  if      (cmd == "forward")   motorForward();
  else if (cmd == "backward")  motorBackward();
  else if (cmd == "left")      motorTurnLeft();
  else if (cmd == "right")     motorTurnRight();
  else if (cmd == "stop")      motorStop();
  else if (cmd.startsWith("sound/")) {
    int n = cmd.substring(6).toInt();
    if (n >= 1 && n <= 7) playSound((R2Sound)n);
  }
  else if (cmd.startsWith("dome/")) {
    int angle = cmd.substring(5).toInt();
    domeSnap(constrain(angle, 0, 180));
  }
}

unsigned long lastRemoteCmdMs     = 0;
const unsigned long REMOTE_TIMEOUT = 5000; // return to autonomous after 5s

// ── Core 1: real-time loop ───────────────────────────────────────
void realtimeLoop(void* param) {
  // Init all real-time systems on Core 1
  initAnimations();
  initSensors();
  initDisplay();
  initMotion();
  initVoiceSystem();

  for (;;) {
    if (!bootDone) { showBootScreen(); vTaskDelay(10); continue; }

    float dist   = readDistance();
    bool  motion = checkMotion();

    // Share state with Wi-Fi (Core 0 reads these)
    sharedDistance  = dist;
    sharedMotion    = motion;
    sharedDomeAngle = currentDomeAngle;

    // ── Remote command takes priority ──────────────────────────
    bool remoteActive = (millis() - lastRemoteCmdMs < REMOTE_TIMEOUT);

    if (remoteCommandPending) {
      String cmd = remoteCommand;
      remoteCommandPending = false;
      processRemoteCommand(cmd);
      lastRemoteCmdMs = millis();
      remoteActive    = true;
    }

    // ── Autonomous behaviour (when no remote command) ─────────
    if (!remoteActive) {
      if (dist < 30.0f) {
        domeSnap(90);
        motorStop();
      } else if (motion) {
        domeSnap(45);
      } else {
        domeIdleSweep();
      }
    }

    // ── Display + lights + voice (always) ─────────────────────
    if (dist < 15.0f)    showAlertScreen();
    else if (motion)     showMotionScreen();
    else                 showIdleScreen(dist);

    updateAnimationsSensors(dist, motion);
    updateVoice(dist, motion, currentState);

    vTaskDelay(10); // 10ms tick — 100Hz loop
  }
}

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  Serial.println("╔═══════════════════════════════╗");
  Serial.println("║   R2-D2 — COMPLETE BUILD      ║");
  Serial.println("║   ESP32-S3 N16R8              ║");
  Serial.println("╚═══════════════════════════════╝");

  // Start Wi-Fi server on Core 0
  xTaskCreatePinnedToCore(
    wifiTask,       // function
    "WiFiTask",     // name
    8192,           // stack size (bytes)
    NULL,           // parameter
    1,              // priority
    NULL,           // task handle
    0               // core: 0
  );

  // Start real-time loop on Core 1
  xTaskCreatePinnedToCore(
    realtimeLoop,   // function
    "RTLoop",       // name
    8192,           // stack size
    NULL,           // parameter
    2,              // priority (higher than Wi-Fi)
    &realtimeTask,  // task handle
    1               // core: 1
  );

  // Arduino loop() runs on Core 1 by default — we've moved everything
  // to FreeRTOS tasks, so loop() is intentionally empty.
}

void loop() {
  vTaskDelay(portMAX_DELAY); // yield forever — all work done in tasks
}

The Final Master Wiring Diagram 🧭

C-3PO takes a deep breath and presents the complete diagram with a formality appropriate to the occasion.

C-3PO: "The final wiring diagram. Every component. Every wire. Every protection circuit. Every power rail. Downloadable as PDF. Printable. Frameable. I am... genuinely proud of this."

R2-D2 COMPLETE BUILD — Master Wiring Diagram
ESP32-S3 N16R8 | All Systems
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

POWER RAIL 1 — LOGIC (USB 5V regulated to 3.3V by ESP32-S3):
  USB 5V → ESP32-S3 VIN
  ESP32 3V3 output → (nothing external — 3.3V stays internal)
  ESP32 GND → Common GND rail

POWER RAIL 2 — 5V PERIPHERALS (L298N internal regulator from 9V):
  9V Battery (+) → L298N: VS
  9V Battery (−) → GND (common with ESP32 GND!)
  L298N 5V out → Servo VCC (red), 74AHCT125 VCC, DFPlayer VCC
  L298N GND → Servo GND, 74AHCT125 GND, DFPlayer GND

I2C BUS (conflict-resolved: SDA=GPIO4, SCL=GPIO5):
  ESP32 GPIO4 (SDA) ──── OLED SDA
  ESP32 GPIO5 (SCL) ──── OLED SCL
  ESP32 3V3         ──── OLED VCC (via 100µF cap to GND)

NEOPIXEL DOME (5V logic via level shifter):
  ESP32 GPIO6 ──── 74AHCT125 A1 ──── NeoPixel DIN
  L298N 5V    ──── NeoPixel 5V + 100µF cap+
  GND         ──── NeoPixel GND + 100µF cap−
  (470Ω resistor between 74AHCT125 Y1 output and NeoPixel DIN)

HC-SR04 ULTRASONIC (5V sensor, voltage divider on ECHO):
  ESP32 GPIO9  (TRIG) ──────────────── HC-SR04 TRIG
  HC-SR04 ECHO (5V)   ── 10kΩ ──┬──── ESP32 GPIO10
                                 └──── 20kΩ ──── GND

PIR MOTION SENSOR (5V output, voltage divider):
  L298N 5V    ──── PIR HC-SR501 VCC
  GND         ──── PIR HC-SR501 GND
  PIR OUT (5V) ── 10kΩ ──┬──── ESP32 GPIO2
                          └──── 20kΩ ──── GND

SERVO — DOME ROTATION (5V signal via level shifter):
  ESP32 GPIO14 ──── 74AHCT125 A2 ──── Servo signal (orange)
  L298N 5V     ──── Servo VCC (red)
  GND          ──── Servo GND (brown)

L298N MOTOR DRIVER:
  ESP32 GPIO25 ──── L298N IN1
  ESP32 GPIO26 ──── L298N IN2
  ESP32 GPIO27 ──── L298N ENA (PWM)
  ESP32 GPIO32 ──── L298N IN3
  ESP32 GPIO33 ──── L298N IN4
  ESP32 GPIO34 ──── L298N ENB (PWM)
  L298N OUT1,2 ──── DC Motor Left
  L298N OUT3,4 ──── DC Motor Right

DFPLAYER MINI AUDIO:
  L298N 5V    ──── DFPlayer VCC + 10µF cap+
  GND         ──── DFPlayer GND + 10µF cap−
  ESP32 GPIO17 ── (1kΩ) ─── DFPlayer RX
  DFPlayer TX ──── ESP32 GPIO16
  DFPlayer SPK1 ── Speaker +
  DFPlayer SPK2 ── Speaker −

Color code (master):
  RED    = 5V / VIN
  ORANGE = 9V battery rail
  PURPLE = 3.3V logic
  BLACK  = GND (common rail)
  GREEN  = NeoPixel data (level-shifted 5V)
  BLUE   = Servo signal (level-shifted 5V)
  YELLOW = Motor direction pins (IN1-IN4)
  WHITE  = Motor PWM enable pins (ENA, ENB)
  CYAN   = I2C SDA (GPIO4)
  PINK   = I2C SCL (GPIO5)
  BROWN  = HC-SR04 TRIG (GPIO9)
  VIOLET = HC-SR04 ECHO with divider (GPIO10)
  GREY   = PIR output with divider (GPIO2)
  MAROON = DFPlayer UART (TX=GPIO17, RX=GPIO16)

COMPLETE Connection Table:
┌──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ From                 │ To                                       │
├──────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ USB 5V               │ ESP32-S3 VIN                             │
│ 9V Battery (+)       │ L298N VS                                 │
│ 9V Battery (−)       │ L298N GND + ESP32 GND (common)           │
│ L298N 5V             │ Servo VCC, 74AHCT125 VCC, DFPlayer VCC   │
│ ESP32 GPIO4 (SDA)    │ OLED SDA                                 │
│ ESP32 GPIO5 (SCL)    │ OLED SCL                                 │
│ ESP32 3V3            │ OLED VCC (+ 100µF cap to GND)            │
│ ESP32 GPIO6          │ 74AHCT125 A1 → (470Ω) → NeoPixel DIN     │
│ ESP32 GPIO9 (TRIG)   │ HC-SR04 TRIG                             │
│ HC-SR04 ECHO (5V)    │ 10k+20k divider → ESP32 GPIO10           │
│ L298N 5V             │ PIR VCC                                  │
│ PIR OUT (5V)         │ 10k+20k divider → ESP32 GPIO2            │
│ ESP32 GPIO14         │ 74AHCT125 A2 → Servo signal (orange)     │
│ ESP32 GPIO25         │ L298N IN1                                │
│ ESP32 GPIO26         │ L298N IN2                                │
│ ESP32 GPIO27 (PWM)   │ L298N ENA                                │
│ ESP32 GPIO32         │ L298N IN3                                │
│ ESP32 GPIO33         │ L298N IN4                                │
│ ESP32 GPIO34 (PWM)   │ L298N ENB                                │
│ L298N OUT1,2         │ DC Motor A                               │
│ L298N OUT3,4         │ DC Motor B                               │
│ L298N 5V             │ DFPlayer VCC + 10µF cap+                 │
│ ESP32 GPIO17 (TX2)   │ 1kΩ → DFPlayer RX                        │
│ DFPlayer TX          │ ESP32 GPIO16 (RX2)                       │
│ DFPlayer SPK1        │ Speaker terminal 1                       │
│ DFPlayer SPK2        │ Speaker terminal 2                       │
└──────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚡ Critical Notes:
  - 9V battery GND MUST connect to ESP32 GND (common ground)
  - 74AHCT125 handles BOTH NeoPixel AND servo level-shift
  - HC-SR04 ECHO and PIR output: voltage dividers MANDATORY on ESP32-S3
  - DFPlayer RX 1kΩ resistor is mandatory
  - OLED I2C now on GPIO4(SDA)/GPIO5(SCL) — conflict with GPIO9 resolved

C-3PO: "Download the PDF. All twenty-three connection rows. Printed. Laminated if possible. This is the complete blueprint of R2-D2."

Final Compile 🚀 

✓ Compilation successful
  Board:        ESP32-S3 N16R8
  Files:        r2d2-main.ino + 6 headers
  Binary:       634,512 bytes (9.1% of 16MB Flash)
  RAM:          Used 52,416 bytes (16.0% of 327KB)
  PSRAM:        8.0 MB free
  Wi-Fi:        Access Point mode — SSID: "R2-D2"
  Dual-core:    Core 0: Wi-Fi/HTTP | Core 1: Real-time control

Click Upload.

The ESP32-S3 boots. The OLED shows "R2-D2 ONLINE." The startup fanfare plays from the speaker. The dome LEDs breathe. The servo sweeps. The Serial Monitor shows:

╔═══════════════════════════════╗
║   R2-D2 — COMPLETE BUILD      ║
║   ESP32-S3 N16R8              ║
╚═══════════════════════════════╝
OLED projector online!
Dome servo centred.
Motor driver online.
DFPlayer Mini online!
R2-D2 says: [sound 5]
[WiFi] AP started: R2-D2
[WiFi] IP: 192.168.4.1
[WiFi] HTTP server started — connect to R2-D2 network
All systems ONLINE. R2-D2 is ready.
Distance: 95 cm — SAFE

Pick up your phone. Connect to the "R2-D2" Wi-Fi network. Password: beepboop. Open a browser. Navigate to 192.168.4.1.

A simple control page appears. Blue on black. R2-D2's face.

Tap "Forward."

The wheels turn.

Tap "Happy."

R2's happy whistle plays.

Tap "Dome ◄."

The dome snaps left.

The Final Milestone 🚩 

Milestone: "R2-D2 COMPLETE — Episode 8 Final Build"

Every episode. Every system. Every milestone. All saved. All restorable.

The Complete Droid 🤖

The workshop is quiet. The crew stands in a circle around R2-D2 — the completed droid. Dome rotating. Lights breathing. OLED projecting. Speaker quiet for now, waiting.

OBI-WAN: "You see what has been built. Not just a robot. Not just a collection of components. The process itself is the lesson — the way Codey lowered every barrier between the idea and the creation. You described. It wrote. You compiled. It fixed. You drew. It diagrammed. Each episode, a little more complex. Each episode, possible."

LUKE: "Eight episodes. I went from blinking an LED to a Wi-Fi controlled droid with dome lights, sensors, a display, motors, and audio. And I'm not a programmer."

HAN: "Kid, I've seen the Millennium Falcon held together with less intentionality than this thing. And the Falcon can make the Kessel Run."

R2-D2 beeps. Loudly. Repeatedly. Proudly.

C-3PO: "He says — and I am translating precisely — 'I told you all it would work.'"

A long pause.

C-3PO: "He also says thank you. Which, from Artoo, is quite remarkable."

Obi-Wan smiles.

OBI-WAN: "The Force will be with you. Always."

R2-D2 plays his startup fanfare one more time. The dome lights sweep blue-white. The OLED displays: "MISSION COMPLETE."

The Series Map — What We Built 🗺️

Episode System Board Codey Feature Spotlight

2 Piezo sound Arduino UNO R3 Smart Library Picker, tone(), Serial Monitor
